message:
>Women should keep the good stuff for the one who matters.

Why?

Also what if I wear revealing clothes because I like the feel of air on my skin, the feeling of being free of all the trouble of buttons, folds, zippers, pockets, etc.? I do not have to wear revealing clothes because I crave attention: I have all the attention I could want. I could be wearing revealing clothes because I happen to like how I look in them, I am producing my own validation. Or because it's 110 degrees Fahrenheit with 80% humidity and I'll be damned if I'm sweating just because the alternative might be eye-catching.

And if you need to leave the city for anything? Public transit system is only good enough if you never need to leave the city for any reason. Which should be the norm, but the public transit system is often horribly underdeveloped in most of the country.

>thats a complete lie. my guns only shoot steel and put holes in paper, just like the majority of guns.


So you NEED to put holes in paper and steel? If that's all you wanted a gun for you could go to a shooting range a rent one for an hour and go nuts.

>

>is this supposed to be relevant? 

It's relevant because it isn't required in every state to have a gun license and to register your guns. Yet it is for cars. And before you say its easy to get a licence to drive a car, I think that should be more difficult too.

>Racial tensions are on the rise, according to most Americans in this poll.

According to a cherrypicked poll run by an organization with clear political leanings to creating a narrative of racial tensions... Not exactly a very useful poll. 

>While it's good that there's been an increase of focus on the killing of unarmed minorities, the fact that there's still so many killing of unarmed minorities is depressing.

I assume you're referring to police shootings. This is a myth. Statistically, white men are still more likely to be shot and killed by the police. More white men are shot and killed by police, and more white men are shot and killed by police per capita. In the instances where minorities are shot and killed by the police, it is usually because they are resisting arrest or doing something threatening. The media likes to dwell on videos where black men get shot, but they'll tend to be quiet about videos like [these](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Ooa7wOKHhg). [Or these](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KgO7MJNO3oo). Or any of the majority of police shootings. It's just a spun tale created by the left in their political game. Look into actual statistics and the full context of the incidents and this issue will become far less alarming. 

>Furthermore, we're separating families at the border. 

Another myth. Let's put aside how illegal immigration is a criminal act detrimental to the country and how the parents are irresponsible for dragging children into criminal activity. The families in question were separated for all of a few hours and then reunited. The reason that this happens is because in these cases it is uncertain whether it is an actual family, or a case of human trafficking. However, the media likes to run with the "separating families" bit and disregard all the other facts for the sake of their narrative.

>There is a large scale conspiracy theory that's actually doing real harm. 

I have no idea what conspiracy you're talking about or how it's doing widespread harm. Care to elaborate? Also, a general distrust of the government is a healthy thing. 

>Look, be conservative or be liberal, but this isn't dealing in facts. It's insanity.

Sounds pretty crazy to me too. Got any hard data on how many people, nationwide, believe it, and any studies done on any measurable harm this particular case has done to the country? After all, we want to work on facts, not speculation, as your conspiracy article states.

>There's also currently a president that has a cult around him. Hell, you can see it over at /r/The_Donald and in most cases also on /r/conservative.

There are crazy people on the internet. This has always been since the internet has formed, and this will always be so long as it exists. Crazy people on the internet is not a measure of the state of the nation. 

>Eight intelligence agencies believe that Russia has interfered in our elections.

First, these intelligence agencies *said* that Russia has interfered, but any actual information on what Russia did, how they did it, or what effect it had is being kept from the public, if it even exists. All they're saying is, "Hacking." Hacking what? The ballots? Classified documents? Maybe Russia did, maybe they didn't, but until the evidence is put forward we have no reason to believe it. Washington is a circus right now, and the politics are like Game of Thrones. You've gotta go with what that conspiracy article of yours says and withhold judgement until evidence is presented... even if it's a government agency. 

> GOP refuse to fund election security.

No, they refuse to *increase* funding on election security with this particular bill. I may sound like I'm being nit-picky, but congressional bills are very long, very complicated documents. Election security is already being funded, and the bill in question may not be allocating funds in a way that the GOP believes is fiscally responsible, or even at all effective. I don't know the specifics, I haven't read the bill, and I haven't looked at the minutes of the bill proposal, so it's hard to say exactly why. 

>we have people that still question vaccines, whether the Earth is flat, and whether climate change is real.

The only one of those that is a real issue is climate change, and even then, most people who argue "against" climate change are really just debating whether or not the effects will be as detrimental as some people predict they will be. What's happening is a cost/benefit analysis of what would be required to reduce emissions by a meaningful amount vs. the hit American industry would take as a result. Aside from a very *very* tiny minority of crazies, nobody is seriously denying that climate change is a thing. The left likes to paint it that way, though, to make the right look bad. 

>Income inequality is growing. What good is a "growing economy" when college grads are drowning in debt, not buying homes or cars, and much of the growth is "tricking down" to the elite of the elite anyway?

Well, unemployment is at an long-time low, the job market is booming, wages are going up... It's the perfect time for college grads to carpe diem. As someone who got a Masters degree out of pocket, I have quite a bit to say about folks who are "drowning in debt." Mostly, it's their own poor decision making and they're suffering for it, but I suppose that's an argument for another day. If there's one good thing Trump has done, it's boost our economy, so there's no worries there. 

So, I'd say, all things considered, America is in fantastic shape, especially compared to Europe right now. The problem is that the mainstream media loves crises. The left *and* the right. It gets them viewers. I noticed that every source you linked was from a left-leaning mainstream source, and the narrative they want you to take in is that the country is falling apart. It makes for a good story, and so you'll watch their television show. If you truly have an open mind, I'd recommend looking at what Ben Shapiro and Steven Crowder have to say about these issues. It's very important to balance out what you take in.

Come on. @worn-bumper, I think you were way more on point with your original post, even if I didn't agree with everything you said. This kind of comment just makes me facepalm.

@minimum-rook, I'm so glad you called out @extended-supernova for that ridiculous comment. I mean, come on, reducing Europe's cultural advantages to the number of black people? That's just ignorant. You ignored the questions /u/e36 asked you.  Do you have a SO or children to care for?  Because it sounds like it's just yourself, with a lot of freetime.  That makes it easy to just say "everyone else should be able to do what I do".  But not everyone else is not in the same situation as you are.  Just because someone does not have any extra time to work does not automatically mean they are "just wasting away in front of the TV or the computer".  

Furthermore, let's just say for shits that you do increase the work hours per week.  There are living and breathing studies that, long term, this is awful for the overall health of a society.  Look at Japan, for example.  The suicide rates are astronical for an otherwise successful country.  Why?  Because the Japanese work ethic, while sometimes admirable, is brutal and drives many people into the ground.

That's pretty insane, and must come at a pretty hefty cost in terms of free time and social life.  Do you have a significant other, or kids?  How much time do you actually spend on yourself, and do you really think that it's reasonable?

>I am not open to changing my view based off of the following: 1) whether or not the above "earned" their wealth or not (I don't care and it's not relevant; if they were more productive, good, they should feel proud of that, but it doesn't change the imperative to address inequality), 

Please please just consider why it matters if you create wealth versus steal it.

When wealth is created (in the case of Bill Gates), it represents value given to billions of people, that made them richer to to the tune of many multiples of what Bill Gates got. A creator-billionaire *decreases* the gap between rich and poor!

You may not believe it, but if we added all the value Microsoft or Google has given the world in terms of non-cash value/wealth, such as Windows and iPhones, if you put a dollar value to those intangibles that the rest of the world now possesses, then the gap does not look so horrendous.

Just consider - you have more computing power in your pocket than what the US used to send Apollo to the moon. Google Maps has saved trillions upon trillions of minutes in saved time for travellers.

The gap created by *theft* however (see Russia, Bangladesh, DPRK) is when the gap is a problem and unfair. The people got nothing out of it.

Gates of course can save more lives with his money than some tinpot dictator who does't understand the value of money. And Gates *does* (see Gates Foundation), because creators are *good people* and thieves are *scumbags*. 

$1 in the hand of different people has a different value! It's only as good as the person who holds it!

@oily-differential, that's a pretty simplistic view of how wealth creation works. You think Bill Gates just magically created value out of thin air and everyone else is just a beneficiary of his genius? Newsflash: the wealth gap isn't just about who's "good" or "bad", it's about a system that's rigged against the majority. Gates' success is built on the backs of countless underpaid workers, tax breaks, and government subsidies. Don't pretend like he's some kind of hero just because he's decided to throw some of his wealth at charity.

message:
> It's not destroying the framework, it's deconstructing it. 

Use whatever verb you want. The end result is the erosion of the idea that women are capable of consenting to sex. This is what modern feminism is buying us.   

>That's rape culture.

We live in a society in which rape is among the most abhorrent crimes that can be committed. Even other convicted criminals will punish harshly those convicted of rape. **There is no such thing as 'rape culture'.** At least not in the West. What was ACTUALLY wrong with sex relations in the past is that women were not allowed to express a desire to have sex. It's the reason why 'baby it's cold outside' has been getting a lot of flak. The woman in the song can't just come out and say 'fuck me'. She has to pretend to not want it. 

What we need is not to 'smash the patriarchy' or 'bring down rape culture', it's to treat women as though they have **agency**. Somehow, modern feminism is against that idea.

>Modern feminism is infantalizing women and destroying any framework for functional consent. The dream of 'all sex is rape' is one step closer. 

Maybe it is a few steps closer.

It's not destroying the framework, it's deconstructing it. The idea of deconstruction is to take a system apart and then build a better one on top of the parts that are good. Sometimes this means taking out a good piece and then realizing you should put it back.

This deconstruction is not unwarranted, since the culture of rape and consent before feminism was, let's say *not so great*. In the movie golfing er bond literally pins down a woman and forcibly kisses her, all the while the audience is supposed to be rooting for him. *That's* rape culture. That kind of thing is not the norm anymore thankfully, but that's thanks to those who cared to deconstruct and criticise the societal norms at the time.

So it can seem like backward progress. But it's just like correcting bad writing, you have to "move backward" by erasing bad stuff to make room for the good stufd. So be patient, and advocate not for the fact that the system is "good enough as it is". It may be fine for you, but not for others, and that means that the system is not ideal on the whole. If you don't want to participate in deconstruction, then educate yourself on the legitimate grievances of those who have them, and think not about what norms to take away, but what ideal norms should replace them. This is tough and will take research and soul searching, but it is the course which must be taken, so if you're not on it, you're not helping anyone.

@urbane-fader, I see your point about deconstructing the system to build a better one, but @bare-brass has a valid concern that it might be taken too far and undermine the idea of consent. Can't we find a balance between promoting healthy relationships and respecting women's agency?

@balanced-download, yeah I think we can find a balance, but it's hard when some people are so extreme with their views. Like, I get that @bare-brass is worried about consent, but @urbane-fader has a point about how messed up things were in the past. Maybe we should focus on educating people about what consent really means instead of tearing down the whole system?

@acidic-plate, I completely agree with you on education being key. It's astonishing how often the concept of consent is misunderstood or downplayed. Perhaps we should push for more comprehensive sex education that emphasizes mutual respect and clear communication. @urbane-fader's point about deconstructing harmful norms is well-taken, but as @balanced-download said, we do need to be mindful of balance to ensure we're not inadvertently undermining important principles like consent.

message:
>Women acknowledged that men have unfair societal expectations laid upon them too, including a pressure not to show emotions, stigmas against being around children or being a single father, and even workplace prejudice in some places (including in my profession in early childhood education which seems to be 90% white females in most schools in my district despite the student body only having about 25-30% white females).

Feminists still acknowledge these facts, but refuse to call it "reverse sexism" or otherwise legitimize a reactionary ideology such as MRA.  Rather, the liberal way to state these facts is to call it the "damage patriarchy does to men" or the problems of "toxic masculinity".

>by trying to guarantee equality of outcome instead of equality of opportunity, we throw merit considerations out the window and enact what is plainly a form of gender prejudice in the appointment process.

So here you are favoring a right-wing consideration ("merit") over a left-wing consideration (equality of outcome).  It is no wonder that you see pushback from liberal feminists.  Right-wing feminists will of course see that as reasonable if the definition of merit is a reasonable definition in their worldview.  There are of course so many ways to construct "merit", and it's nearly impossible to avoid putting one's own ideology into that definition.

> The bible is fairly clear about it's logical basis- it was known at the time that Jesus did miracles and resurrected so you should believe in it.

Not quite. The Bible derives its authority from its being the revealed Word of God. Christ as a manifestation of that is only a part of it, albeit an essential one for Christians. The historicity of the Bible is secondary to its being revealed truth.

Now, how we are to take these truths is a matter of some dispute in any circle, be it religious or otherwise. So there's also the extra difficulty of attacking it in principle and not just in one application.

message:
>Acts 2:22-36 Ye men of Israel, hear these words; Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved of God among you by miracles and wonders and signs, which God did by him in the midst of you, as ye yourselves also know: Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain: Whom God hath raised up, having loosed the pains of death: because it was not possible that he should be holden of it. For David speaketh concerning him, I foresaw the Lord always before my face, for he is on my right hand, that I should not be moved...Men and brethren, let me freely speak unto you of the patriarch David, that he is both dead and buried, and his sepulchre is with us unto this day. Therefore being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him, that of the fruit of his loins, according to the flesh, he would raise up Christ to sit on his throne; He seeing this before spake of the resurrection of Christ, that his soul was not left in hell, neither his flesh did see corruption. This Jesus hath God raised up, whereof we all are witnesses. Therefore being by the right hand of God exalted, and having received of the Father the promise of the Holy Ghost, he hath shed forth this, which ye now see and hear... Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly, that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ.

The bible is fairly clear about it's logical basis- it was known at the time that Jesus did miracles and resurrected so you should believe in it. 

The main difficulty is we don't have access to extensive contemporary records and such of Jesus's time to prove or disprove this assertion. 

The assertion is logical- Jesus did lots of miracles and taught good things, so by following him you can get some divine aid and good teaching, and if you don't believe look at the evidence. There's just a missing link in the evidence chain.
